就在Facebook的争议似乎已经达到顶峰的时候，该公司现在似乎正在阻碍种族灭绝调查，而且它正在利用美国法律这样做。
西非国家冈比亚正在寻求追究缅甸对罗辛亚人的种族灭绝指控的责任，罗辛亚人是一个少数民族和宗教少数群体。2016年和2017年，缅甸士兵及其文职代理人屠杀了罗辛亚男子、妇女和儿童，强奸了妇女和女孩，将村庄夷为平地，迫使80多万人逃往邻国孟加拉国。
Facebook在这些暴行中扮演的角色并不是新闻。2018年，Facebook承认它在缅甸被用来“煽动分裂和煽动线下暴力”，这个社交媒体平台在缅甸是如此无处不在，以至于往往是互联网的代名词。该公司委托的一份独立报告也记录了同样的情况，联合国任命的独立实况调查员也是如此。
作为回应，Facebook删除了缅甸军方总司令敏昂莱大将以及其他军方官员和组织的账户。仅在2018年，它就关闭了许多试图煽动对罗辛亚人的暴力的网络，删除了484页、157个账户和17个“协调不真实的行为”的团体。
值得称赞的是，Facebook保留了它删除的数据和内容，该公司承诺清理其行为。2018年，一名公司代表在一份官方声明中表示：“我们知道，我们需要做更多工作，以确保我们在缅甸成为一支向善的力量。”
今年6月，冈比亚向美国联邦法院提交申请，要求Facebook提供信息，帮助冈比亚在国际法院(ICJ)追究缅甸的责任。具体地说，冈比亚正在从缅甸军方官员那里寻求文件和通信，以及Facebook删除和保存的数百个其他页面和账户的信息。冈比亚还在寻求与Facebook对此事的内部调查有关的文件，以及Facebook一名相关高管的证词。所有这些信息都可能有助于证明缅甸的种族灭绝意图。
早在5月份，冈比亚就向美国法院提交了类似的针对Twitter的申请。此案很快就消失了，因为冈比亚在提交申请后不久就撤回了申请，想必是因为推特同意合作。
不是脸书。本月早些时候，该公司提交了对冈比亚申请的反对意见。Facebook表示，这一要求“非常宽泛”，而且“过于侵扰或负担过重”。这家社交媒体巨头呼吁美国哥伦比亚特区地区法院拒绝这一申请，称冈比亚未能“足够具体地识别账户”。
冈比亚实际上相当具体，甚至列出了17名官员，两个军事单位和数十页和账户。
Facebook还质疑这样一个事实，即冈比亚正在寻找可以追溯到2012年的信息，显然没有认识到当年针对罗辛亚人的两波类似暴行，而且种族灭绝意图不是自发的，而是随着时间的推移而积累起来的。
数据的管理和分析对Facebook来说并不新鲜。这是一项核心能力。该公司每天分析数十亿用户的数据点，将从个人帖子、聊天和照片中收集的信息货币化。这就是Facebook的商业模式。现在，该公司声称，对它来说，共享已经删除并保留的特定数量账户的数据是不合理的，也是“繁重的”。这个论点不仅没有说服力，而且在道德上令人震惊。
但Facebook争论的症结在于，称冈比亚要求法院发出传票违反了美国法律，特别是违反了《存储通信法》(SCA)的一节。这项重要的联邦法律禁止社交媒体公司心血来潮地向第三方发布通信和数据。
事情是这样的：法律旨在保护个人隐私，而不是保护国家行为者的非法行为。根据SCA，它保护的隐私也有例外，包括当政府拥有有效的传票时。Facebook可以泄露通讯来保护自己的权利，其中包括执行服务条款和阻止平台上的非法行为的权利。
此外，如果在社交媒体上共享的信息是供公众查看的，政制事务局局长不要求公司将这些信息保密。Facebook撤下的许多与缅甸罗辛亚种族灭绝有关的页面和账户都是公开的。
Facebook可能会说，它担心开创一个危险的先例，但通过美国联邦法院分享种族灭绝意图的信息似乎正是该公司应该设立的“先例”，即阻止国家行为者将其平台用于犯罪目的。更不用说，自愿遵守冈比亚的要求不会创造任何法律先例，只是公司内部的先例。
冈比亚预计将于8月18日在法庭上回应Facebook的反对。无论这一请求如何展开，美国法律永远不应该被用来保护种族灭绝的肇事者。如果Facebook现在不做正确的事情，那么国会应该做出决定。
美国立法者应该迅速通过一项对SCA的修正案，要求社交媒体公司与官方机构和国际法庭的诉讼当事人分享相关信息，试图追究种族灭绝和大规模暴行罪行的肇事者的责任。法律应对任何未在被适当要求时泄露此类信息的行为施加民事责任。
除此之外，就目前而言，反对种族灭绝的立场可能和删除你的Facebook账户一样容易。

Just when it seemed like Facebook’s controversies might have peaked, the company now appears to be obstructing  a genocide investigation, and it’s using U.S. law to do it.
  The West African nation The Gambia is seeking  to hold Myanmar accountable for charges of  genocide against the Rohingya people, an ethnic and religious minority. In 2016 and 2017, Myanmar soldiers and their civilian proxies  massacred Rohingya men, women and children, raped women and girls and razed villages, forcing more than 800,000 to flee into neighboring Bangladesh.
  Facebook’s role in these atrocities isn’t news. In 2018, Facebook  acknowledged it was used to “foment division and incite offline violence” in Myanmar, where the social media platform is so ubiquitous it’s often synonymous with the internet. An independent report commissioned by the company documented the same, as did  independent fact-finders appointed by the U.N.
  In response, Facebook  took down the account of the commander-in-chief of the Myanmar military,  Senior General Min Aung Hlaing, and other military officials and organizations. In 2018 alone it shut down numerous networks that sought to incite violence against Rohingya, removing 484 pages, 157 accounts, and 17 groups for “ coordinated inauthentic behavior.”
  To its credit, Facebook preserved the data and content it took down, and the company committed to cleaning up its act. “We know we need to do more to ensure we are a force for good in Myanmar,” a company representative said in an  official statement in 2018.
        In June, The Gambia filed an application in U.S. federal court seeking information from Facebook that would help it hold Myanmar accountable at the International Court of Justice (ICJ). Specifically, The Gambia is seeking documents and communications from Myanmar military officials as well as information from hundreds of other pages and accounts that Facebook took down and preserved. The Gambia is also seeking documents related to Facebook’s internal investigations into the matter as well as a deposition of a relevant Facebook executive. All of this information could help to prove  Myanmar’s genocidal intent.
    Back in May, The Gambia filed a similar application in U.S. court against Twitter. The case disappeared quickly because The Gambia pulled its application shortly after submitting it, presumably because Twitter agreed to cooperate.
  Not Facebook. Earlier this month, the company filed its opposition to The Gambia’s application. Facebook said the request is “extraordinarily broad,” as well as “unduly intrusive or burdensome.” Calling on the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia to reject the application, the social media giant says The Gambia fails to “identify accounts with sufficient specificity.”
  The Gambia was actually quite specific, going so far as to name 17 officials, two military units and dozens of pages and accounts.
  Facebook also takes issue with the fact that The Gambia is seeking information dating back to 2012, evidently failing to recognize  two similar waves of atrocities against Rohingya that year, and that genocidal intent isn’t spontaneous, but builds over time.
  The management and  analysis of data is not new to Facebook. It’s a core competency. The company analyzes billions of data points on its users every day, monetizing information collected from personal posts, chats and photos. That is  Facebook’s business model. Now the company claims it’s unreasonable or “burdensome” for it to share data on a specific number of accounts that it already removed and preserved. The argument is not only unconvincing; it’s morally appalling.
    But the crux of Facebook’s argument is the claim that The Gambia is asking the court to issue a subpoena in violation of U.S. law, specifically a section of the Stored Communications Act (SCA). This important federal law prevents social media companies from releasing communications and data to third parties on a whim.
  Here’s the thing: the law is intended to protect the privacy of individuals, not shield unlawful actions of State actors. Under the SCA, there are exceptions to the privacy it protects, including when the government has a valid subpoena. And Facebook can divulge communications to protect its own rights, which would include its right to enforce its terms of service and deter unlawful behavior on the platform.
  In addition, if information shared on social media was intended for public viewing, the SCA does not require a company to keep that information private. Many of the pages and accounts that  Facebook took down in relation to the Rohingya genocide in Myanmar were public.
      Facebook might say it’s concerned about setting a dangerous precedent, but sharing information of genocidal intent through a U.S. federal court would seem to be precisely the “precedent” the company should want to set, i.e. to deter State actors from using its platform for criminal purposes. Not to mention that voluntarily complying with The Gambia’s request wouldn’t create any legal precedent, only an internal one at the company.
  The Gambia is expected to respond to Facebook’s opposition in court on Aug. 18. Regardless of how this request unfolds, U.S. law should never be used to protect perpetrators of genocide. If Facebook won’t do the right thing now, then Congress should make them.
  U.S. legislators should pass a swift amendment to the SCA that would require social media companies to share relevant information with official bodies and litigants at international tribunals attempting to hold perpetrators of genocide and mass atrocity crimes accountable. The law should impose civil liability for any failure to divulge such information when properly requested.
  Beyond that, for the time being, taking a stand against genocide could be as easy as deleting your Facebook account.

加拿大伦敦最漂亮的小镇现在是世界上第一个零排放压缩空气能源设施的所在地。
废弃的矿井往往会造成真正的环境问题-但在风景如画的戈德里奇小镇深处，一个曾经的盐洞可能只是掌握着安大略省更便宜、更绿色能源未来的关键。
周五，总部位于多伦多的能源和技术公司Aqustor启动了世界上第一个零排放先进压缩空气储存设施，该设施位于梅特兰河附近一个废弃的溶剂矿盐穴，曾经用于生产卤水和食盐。
该公司表示，将利用安大略省海滨小镇深处的地下洞穴和隧道来更好地捕捉原本会被浪费的过剩能源，并将其储存起来供以后使用。
这个最先进的储能设施是世界上仅有的三个储能设施之一，但水力发电公司首席执行官兼联合创始人柯蒂斯·范瓦赫姆表示，戈德里奇是唯一一个碳足迹为零的设施。
他说，这是世界上第一个零排放压缩空气设施。我们不消耗任何其他燃料，只消耗空气和水。
水力发电公司的戈德里奇设施依赖于这样一个事实，即安大略省的发电商产生的电力比我们作为一个省份消耗的电力还要多。当需求较低时，安大略省将这些过剩的电力出售给美国各州，价格往往低于生产成本。​。
​VanWregem说，他的公司的设施利用这些时间充电，然后通过空气压缩机吸收多余的电力，将原本会被浪费的能源装进瓶子里，作为压缩空气供以后使用。
当电网挨饿时，水力发电公司可以释放储存的空气，为系统发电。
当电网再次需要电力时，我们打开一个阀门，空气就出来了，范瓦赫姆说。它通过空气涡轮机将空气回流到大气中，产生能量。
水力发电设施可以保持高达10兆瓦的电力，足以让大约2000户家庭，或者说大约一半的戈德里奇小镇的灯保持亮着大约5个小时。
由于效率低下，该工厂仍损失了约三分之一的储存能量，但与类似容量的电池相比，它的成本不仅是一半，而且该设施的使用寿命为50年，是电池的5至10倍。
范瓦赫姆说，他的公司的技术是一个性价比高的解决方案，解决了安大略省多年来一直在处理的问题。
在安大略省，电费肯定不便宜。弗雷泽研究所(Fraser Institute)2017年的一项研究显示，安大略省的家庭支付的电费是加拿大最高的，多伦多和渥太华的家庭支付的电费往往是加拿大其他主要城市家庭电费的两倍。
更重要的是，安大略省的家庭只能指望他们的家庭账单会攀升。凯瑟琳·韦恩(Kathleen Wynne)领导的前自由党政府2017年泄露的一份内阁备忘录预测，水电价格将在未来十年内大幅飙升。
永利在2018年大选中被选民否决的部分原因是安大略省水电系统的混乱，但自从她的继任者、进步保守党总理道格·福特(Doug Ford)上任以来，安大略省的表现也好不到哪里去。
安大略省被迫向Hydro One前首席执行官梅奥·施密特支付了数不清的数百万奖金和福利。在他瞄准这位高管600万美元的薪水后，福特迫使他离职。
福特似乎也在美国监管机构的决定中发挥了核心作用，监管机构以福特政府的政治干预为由，拒绝了海德鲁第一集团(Hydro One)收购总部位于斯波坎的Avista Corp.的提议。
福特还以花费2.31亿美元扼杀该省的绿色能源合同而闻名。那么，是什么让范瓦赫姆认为他的想法在道格·福特的安大略省具有长期的潜力呢？
他说，显然，你在电网上拥有的风能和太阳能越多，就越有意义进行储存。从长远来看，他们可能会增加更多的风能和太阳能，但在短期内，他们认为没有必要增加额外的能源。
他们真正需要的是在你可以调用的时候出现的峰值容量，这是我们可以提供的那种东西。
范瓦赫姆和他的同事们对他们的技术非常有信心，以至于他们重新向海外扩张，在澳大利亚的阿德莱德建立了一个类似的设施。
人们希望，有一天，当各国政府真正转向风能和太阳能等无碳能源时，他们就能有办法储存能源，即使在太阳不照耀的时候也是如此。

London    Canada's 'prettiest town' is now home to the world's first emission-free compressed air energy facility
  Abandoned mines often cause real environmental problems – but a former salt cavern deep below the picturesque town of Goderich may just hold the key to a cheaper, greener energy future in Ontario.
  On Friday, Toronto-based energy and technology company Hydrostor launched the first zero-emission advanced compressed air storage facility in the world, located on the grounds of a deserted solution mine salt cavern once used in the production of brine and table salt near the Maitland River.
   The company says it will use the subterranean chambers and tunnels deep below the Ontario beach town to capture surplus energy that would otherwise be wasted and store it for later use.
  The state-of-the-art energy storage facility is one of only three in the world, but Hydrostor CEO and co-founder Curtis VanWalleghem said Goderich is the only one that has a zero carbon footprint.
  "This is the world's first emission-free compressed air facility," he said. "We don't consume any other fuel, just air and water."
      Hydrostor's Goderich facility relies on the fact Ontario's electricity producers generate more power than we consume as a province. Ontario sells off this excess electricity to U.S. states when demand is low, often at rates below the cost of production. ​
  ​​​​​​VanWalleghem said his company's facility uses those times to charge up, soaking up the excess power through its air compressor, bottling up what would otherwise be wasted energy as compressed air for later use.
  When the power grid gets hungry, Hydrostor can then release that stored air, generating power for the system.
  "When the grid wants power again we open a valve and the air comes out," VanWalleghem said. "It goes through an air turbine returning the air to the atmosphere, producing power."
      The Hydrostor facility can keep up to 10 megawatts of power, enough to keep the lights on in about 2,000 homes, or approximately half the town of Goderich for about five hours.
  The plant still loses about a third of its stored energy through inefficiencies, but compared to a similar capacity battery, it's not only half the cost, the facility has a service life of 50 years, five to 10 times longer than a battery.
  VanWalleghem said his company's technology is a cost-effective solution to a problem Ontario has been dealing with for years.
      In Ontario, electricity certainly doesn't come cheap. A 2017 study by the Fraser Institute suggests  Ontario families pay the highest electricity bills in Canada, with households in Toronto and Ottawa often paying double the electricity rates of homes in other major Canadian cities.
  What's more, Ontario families can only expect their household bills to climb. A leaked cabinet 2017 memo from the former Liberal government under Kathleen Wynne predicted  hydro rates will undergo a steep surge within the next decade.
  Part of the reason Wynne was turfed by voters in the 2018 election was over the mess that is Ontario's hydro system, but ever since her successor, Progressive Conservative Premier Doug Ford took over, Ontario hasn't fared much better.
  Ontario was forced to pay untold millions in awards and benefits to Mayo Schmidt, the former CEO of Hydro One and a man whom Ford pressured to leave after he targeted the executive's $6-million salary.
      Ford also appeared to play a central role in the decision by U.S. regulators, who  denied Hydro One's proposed takeover of Spokane based Avista Corp., citing political interference by the Ford government.
  Ford is also famous for the fact he  spent $231 million dollars to kill green energy contracts in the province. So what makes VanWalleghem think his idea has long-term potential in Doug Ford's Ontario?
  "Obviously the more wind and solar you have on the grid, the more sense it makes for storage," he said. "Long-term they will likely add more wind and solar but in the short-term they don't see the need for that extra energy."
  "What they do need is that peaking capacity that comes when you can call it and that's the sort of thing we can offer."
  VanWalleghem and his colleagues are so convinced in their technology that they're branching out overseas, building a similar facility in Adelaide, Australia.
  With the hope that one day, when governments truly go to carbon-free energy sources such as wind and solar, they have a way to store the energy, even when the sun isn't shining.
